# xDSL: A Python-native SSA Compiler Framework

[xDSL](http://www.xdsl.dev) is a Python-native compiler framework built around
SSA-based intermediate representations (IRs). Users of xDSL build a compiler by
assembling predefined domain-specific IRs and, optionally, defining their own custom IRs. xDSL uses multi-level IRs, meaning
that during the compilation process, a program will be lowered through several
of these IRs. This allows the implementation of abstraction-specific
optimization passes, similar to the structure of common DSL compilers (such as
Devito, Psyclone, and Firedrake). To simplify the writing of these passes, xDSL
uses a uniform data structure based on SSA, basic blocks, and regions, which
additionally enables the writing of generic passes.

The design of xDSL is influenced by [MLIR](https://mlir.llvm.org/), a compiler
framework developed in C++, that is part of the LLVM project. An inherent
advantage of a close design is the easy interaction between the two frameworks,
making it possible to translate abstractions and programs back and forth. This
results in one big SSA-based abstraction ecosystem that can be worked with
through Python, making analysis through simple scripting languages possible.
Additionally, xDSL can leverage MLIR's code generation and low-level
optimization capabilities.

- [Installation](#installation)
- [Getting Started](#getting-started)
- [xDSL Developer Setup](#xdsl-developer-setup)
  - [Developer Installation](#developer-installation)
  - [Testing](#testing)
  - [Formatting](#formatting)

## Installation

To use xDSL as part of a larger project for developing your own compiler,
just install [xDSL via pip](https://pypi.org/project/xdsl/):

```bash
pip install xdsl
```

*Note:* This version of xDSL is validated against a specific MLIR version,
interoperability with other versions may result in problems. The supported
MLIR version is commit `d401987fe349a87c53fe25829215b080b70c0c1a`.

### Subprojects With Extra Dependencies

xDSL has a number of subprojects, some of which require extra dependencies.
In order to keep the set of dependencies ot a minimum, these extra dependencies have to be
specified explicitly. To install these, use:

``` bash
pip install xdsl[gui,jax,riscv,wgpu,onnx]
```

To install the testing/development dependencies, use:

``` bash
pip install xdsl[dev]
```

These may be useful for projects wanting to replicate the xDSL testing setup.

## Getting Started

Check out the dedicated [Getting Started guide](GETTING_STARTED.md) for a comprehensive tutorial.

To get familiar with xDSL, we recommend starting with our Jupyter notebooks. The
notebooks consist of examples and documentation concerning the core xDSL data
structures and the xDSL's Python-embedded abstraction definition language, as
well as examples of implementing custom compilers, like a database compiler.
There also exists a small documentation showing how to connect xDSL with MLIR
for users interested in that use case.

- [A Database example](https://xdsl.dev/xdsl/lab/index.html?path=database_example.ipynb)
- [A simple introduction](https://xdsl.dev/xdsl/lab/index.html?path=tutorial.ipynb)
- [A DSL for defining new IRs](https://xdsl.dev/xdsl/lab/index.html?path=irdl.ipynb)
- [Connecting xDSL with MLIR](docs/mlir_interoperation.md)

We provide a Makefile containing a lot of common tasks, which might provide
an overview of common actions.

## xDSL Developer Setup

To contribute to the development of xDSL follow the subsequent steps.

### Developer Installation

```bash
git clone https://github.com/xdslproject/xdsl.git
cd xdsl
# set up the venv and install everything
make venv
# activate the venv
source venv/bin/activate
```

### Testing

The xDSL project uses pytest unit tests and LLVM-style filecheck tests. They can
be executed from the root directory:

```bash
# Executes pytests which are located in tests/
pytest

# Executes filecheck tests
lit tests/filecheck

# run all tests using makefile
make tests
```

### Formatting and Typechecking

All python code used in xDSL uses [ruff](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/formatter/) to
format the code in a uniform manner.

To automate the formatting, we use pre-commit hooks from the
[pre-commit](https://pypi.org/project/pre-commit/) package.

```bash
# Install the pre-commit on your `.git` folder
make precommit-install
# to run the hooks:
make precommit
# alternatively, run ruff directly:
ruff format
```

Furthermore, all python code must run through [pyright](https://github.com/microsoft/pyright)
without errors. Pyright can be run on all staged files through the
makefile using `make pyright`.

### Discussion

You can also join the discussion at our [Zulip chat room](https://xdsl.zulipchat.com), kindly supported by community hosting from [Zulip](https://zulip.com/).

is not `b`.), assuming they are in the same region.

modules.name: xdsl.interactive.app
documentation:
An interactive command-line tool to explore compilation pipeline construction.

Execute `xdsl-gui` in your terminal to run it.

Run `textual run xdsl.interactive.app:InputApp --dev` to run in development mode. Please
be sure to install `textual-dev` to run this command.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.constraints.ConstraintVar

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.constraints.ConstraintVar.name
documentation: The variable name. All uses of that name refer to the same variable.

Annotation used in PyRDL to define a constraint variable.
For instance, the following code defines a constraint variable T,
that can then be used in PyRDL:
```python
T = Annotated[PyRDLConstraint, ConstraintVar("T")]
```

documentation:
Erase an operation.
Check that the operation has no uses, and has a parent.
If safe_erase is True, check that the operation has no uses.
Otherwise, replace its uses with ErasedSSAValue.

methods.name: xdsl.rewriter.Rewriter.replace_op
documentation:
Replace an operation with multiple new ones.
If new_results is specified, map the results of the deleted operations with these
SSA values.
Otherwise, use the results of the last operation added.
None elements in new_results are the SSA values to delete.
If safe_erase is False, then operations can be deleted even if they are
still used.

methods.name: xdsl.rewriter.Rewriter.inline_block
documentation:
Move the block operations before another operation.
The block should not be a parent of the operation.

methods.name: xdsl.rewriter.Rewriter.inline_block_at_end
documentation:
Move the block operations to the end of another block.
This block should not be a parent of the block to move to.
The block operations should not use the block arguments.

methods.name: xdsl.rewriter.Rewriter.inline_block_at_start
documentation:
Move the block operations to the start of another block.
This block should not be a parent of the block to move to.
The block operations should not use the block arguments.

methods.name: xdsl.rewriter.Rewriter.inline_block_after
documentation:
Move the block operations after another operation.
The block should not be a parent of the operation.
The block operations should not use the block arguments.

methods.name: xdsl.rewriter.Rewriter.insert_block_after
documentation:
Insert one or multiple blocks after another block.
The blocks to insert should be detached from any region.
The target block should not be contained in the block to insert.

methods.name: xdsl.rewriter.Rewriter.insert_block_before
documentation:
Insert one or multiple block before another block.
The blocks to insert should be detached from any region.
The target block should not be contained in the block to insert.

methods.name: xdsl.rewriter.Rewriter.insert_op
documentation: Insert operations at a certain location in a block.

methods.name: xdsl.rewriter.Rewriter.insert_op_after
documentation: Inserts a new operation after another operation.

methods.name: xdsl.rewriter.Rewriter.insert_op_before
documentation: Inserts a new operation before another operation.

methods.name: xdsl.rewriter.Rewriter.move_region_contents_to_new_regions
documentation: Move the region blocks to a new region.

methods.name: xdsl.rewriter.Rewriter.inline_region_before
documentation: Move the region blocks to an existing region, before `target`.

methods.name: xdsl.rewriter.Rewriter.inline_region_after
documentation: Move the region blocks to an existing region, after `target`.

methods.name: xdsl.rewriter.Rewriter.inline_region_at_start
documentation: Move the region blocks to the start of an existing region.

methods.name: xdsl.rewriter.Rewriter.inline_region_at_end
documentation: Move the region blocks to the end of an existing region.

modules.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser
documentation:
This file contains a lexer and a parser for the MLIR declarative assembly format:
https://mlir.llvm.org/docs/DefiningDialects/Operations/#declarative-assembly-format

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatLexer

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatLexer.lex
documentation: Lex a token from the input, and returns it.

A lexer for the declarative assembly format.
The differences with the MLIR lexer are the following:
* It can parse '`' or '$' as tokens. The token will have the `BARE_IDENT` kind.
* Bare identifiers may also may contain `-`.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.parse_format
documentation:
Parse a declarative format, with the following syntax:
  format ::= directive*
Once the format is parsed, check that it is correct, i.e., that it is
unambiguous and refer to all elements exactly once.

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.verify_directives
documentation:
Check correctness of the declarative format; e.g, chaining variadiclike operand
directives leads to ambiguous parsing, and should raise an error here.

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.add_reserved_attrs_to_directive
documentation:
Add reserved attributes to the attr-dict directive.
These are the attributes that are printed/parsed in other places in the format,
and thus should not be printed in the attr-dict directive.

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.extractors_by_name
documentation: Find out which constraint variables can be inferred from the parsed attributes.

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.verify_operands
documentation:
Check that all operands and operand types are refered at least once, or inferred
from another construct.

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.verify_results
documentation:
Check that all result types are refered at least once, or inferred
from another construct.

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.verify_attr_dict
documentation: Check that the attribute dictionary is present.

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.verify_properties
documentation:
Check that all properties are present, unless `ParsePropInAttrDict` option is
used.

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.verify_regions
documentation: Check that all regions are present.

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.verify_successors
documentation: Check that all successors are present.

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.parse_optional_typeable_variable
documentation:
Parse a variable, if present, with the following format:
  variable ::= `$` bare-ident
The variable should refer to an operand or result.

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.parse_optional_variable
documentation:
Parse a variable, if present, with the following format:
  variable ::= `$` bare-ident
The variable should refer to an operand, attribute, region, or successor.

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.parse_type_directive
documentation:
Parse a type directive with the following format:
  type-directive ::= `type` `(` typeable-directive `)`
`type` is expected to have already been parsed

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.parse_optional_group
documentation:
Parse an optional group, with the following format:
  group ::= `(` then-elements `)` `?`

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.parse_keyword_or_punctuation
documentation:
Parse a keyword or a punctuation directive, with the following format:
  keyword-or-punctuation-directive ::= `\`` (bare-ident | punctuation) `\``

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.parse_typeable_directive
documentation:
Parse a typeable directive, with the following format:
  directive ::= variable

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.parse_format_directive
documentation:
Parse a format directive, with the following format:
  directive ::= `attr-dict`
                | `attr-dict-with-keyword`
                | type-directive
                | keyword-or-punctuation-directive
                | variable

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.create_attr_dict_directive
documentation: Create an attribute dictionary directive, and update the parsing state.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.op_def
documentation: The operation definition we are parsing the format for.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.seen_operands
documentation: The operand variables that are already parsed.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.seen_operand_types
documentation: The operand types that are already parsed.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.seen_result_types
documentation: The result types that are already parsed.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.seen_attributes
documentation: The attributes that are already parsed.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.seen_properties
documentation: The properties that are already parsed.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.seen_regions
documentation: The region variables that are already parsed.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.seen_successors
documentation: The successor variables that are already parsed.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.has_attr_dict
documentation: True if the attribute dictionary has already been parsed.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format_parser.FormatParser.type_resolutions
documentation: Map a variable to a way to infer its type

Parser for the declarative assembly format.
The parser keeps track of the operands, operand types, results, regions, and
attributes that are already parsed, and checks at the end that the provided format
is correct, i.e., that it is unambiguous and refer to all elements exactly once.

classes.name: xdsl.context.MLContext

methods.name: xdsl.context.MLContext.loaded_ops
documentation: Returns all the loaded operations. Not valid across mutations of this object.

methods.name: xdsl.context.MLContext.loaded_attrs
documentation: Returns all the loaded attributes. Not valid across mutations of this object.

methods.name: xdsl.context.MLContext.loaded_dialects
documentation: Returns all the loaded attributes. Not valid across mutations of this object.

methods.name: xdsl.context.MLContext.registered_dialect_names
documentation: Returns the names of all registered dialects. Not valid across mutations of this object.

methods.name: xdsl.context.MLContext.register_dialect
documentation:
Register a dialect without loading it. The dialect is only loaded in the context
when an operation or attribute of that dialect is parsed, or when explicitely
requested with `load_registered_dialect`.

methods.name: xdsl.context.MLContext.load_registered_dialect
documentation: Load a dialect that is already registered in the context.

methods.name: xdsl.context.MLContext.load_dialect
documentation:
Load a dialect. Operation and Attribute names should be unique.
If the dialect is already registered in the context, use
`load_registered_dialect` instead.

methods.name: xdsl.context.MLContext.load_op
documentation: Load an operation definition. Operation names should be unique.

methods.name: xdsl.context.MLContext.load_attr
documentation: Load an attribute definition. Attribute names should be unique.

methods.name: xdsl.context.MLContext.get_optional_op
documentation:
Get an operation class from its name if it exists.
If the operation is not registered, return None unless unregistered operations
are allowed in the context, in which case return an UnregisteredOp.

methods.name: xdsl.context.MLContext.get_op
documentation:
Get an operation class from its name.
If the operation is not registered, raise an exception unless unregistered
operations are allowed in the context, in which case return an UnregisteredOp.

methods.name: xdsl.context.MLContext.get_optional_attr
documentation:
Get an attribute class from its name if it exists.
If the attribute is not registered, return None unless unregistered attributes
are allowed in the context, in which case return an UnregisteredAttr.
Since UnregisteredAttr may be a type (for MLIR compatibility), an
additional flag is required to create an UnregisterAttr that is
also a type.

methods.name: xdsl.context.MLContext.get_attr
documentation:
Get an attribute class from its name.
If the attribute is not registered, raise an exception unless unregistered
attributes are allowed in the context, in which case return an UnregisteredAttr.
Since UnregisteredAttr may be a type (for MLIR compatibility), an
additional flag is required to create an UnregisterAttr that is
also a type.

class_variables.name: xdsl.context.MLContext._registered_dialects
documentation:
A dictionary of all registered dialects that are not yet loaded. This is used to
only load the respective Python files when the dialect is actually used.
Contains structures for operations/attributes registration.

methods.name: xdsl.dialects.affine.ForOp.verify_
documentation: TODO this requires the ImplicitAffineTerminator trait instead of
NoTerminator
gh issue: https://github.com/xdslproject/xdsl/issues/1149

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.affine.IfOp
documentation: https://mlir.llvm.org/docs/Dialects/Affine/#affineif-affineaffineifop

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.affine.ParallelOp
documentation: https://mlir.llvm.org/docs/Dialects/Affine/#affineparallel-affineaffineparallelop

modules.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format
documentation:
This file contains the data structures necessary for the parsing and printing
of the MLIR declarative assembly format defined at
https://mlir.llvm.org/docs/DefiningDialects/Operations/#declarative-assembly-format .

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.ParsingState
documentation:
State carried during the parsing of an operation using the declarative assembly
format.
It contains the elements that have already been parsed.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.PrintingState

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.PrintingState.last_was_punctuation
documentation: Was the last element parsed a punctuation.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.PrintingState.should_emit_space
documentation:
Should the printer emit a space before the next element.
Depending on the directive, the space might not be printed
(for instance for some punctuations).

State carried during the printing of an operation using the declarative assembly
format.
It contains information on the last token, to know if a space should be emitted.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.FormatProgram

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.FormatProgram.from_str
documentation:
Create the assembly format data program from its string representation.
This might raise a ParseError exception if the string is invalid.

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.FormatProgram.parse
documentation:
Parse the operation with this format.
The given operation type is expected to be the operation type represented by
the operation definition passed to the FormatParser that created this
FormatProgram.

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.FormatProgram.resolve_operand_types
documentation:
Use the inferred type resolutions to fill missing operand types from other parsed
types.

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.FormatProgram.resolve_result_types
documentation:
Use the inferred type resolutions to fill missing result types from other parsed
types.

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.FormatProgram.print
documentation:
Print the operation with this format.
The given operation is expected to be defined using the operation definition
passed to the FormatParser that created this FormatProgram.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.FormatProgram.stmts
documentation: The statements composing the program. They are executed in order.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.FormatProgram.extractors
documentation: Extractors for all type variables from the parsing state.

The toplevel data structure of a declarative assembly format program.
It is used to parse and print an operation.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.Directive
documentation: An assembly format directive

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.AnchorableDirective

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.AnchorableDirective.is_present
documentation: Check if the directive is present in the input.
Base class for Directive usable as anchors to optional groups.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.FormatDirective
documentation: A format directive for operation format.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.OptionallyParsableDirective

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.OptionallyParsableDirective.parse_optional
documentation: Try parsing the directive and return True if it was present.

Base class for Directive that can be optionally parsed.
Those are the ones usable as first element of an optional group.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.VariadicLikeFormatDirective

methods.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.VariadicLikeFormatDirective.set_empty
documentation:
Set the appropriate field of the parsing state to be empty.
Used when a variable appears in an optional group which is not parsed.

A directive which parses/prints multiple objects separated by commas.
Such directives can not be followed by comma literals.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.TypeableDirective
documentation: Directives which can be used to set or get types.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.VariadicTypeableDirective
documentation: Directives which can set or get multiple types.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.TypeDirective
documentation:
A directive which parses the type of a typeable directive, with format:
  type-directive ::= type(typeable-directive)

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.VariadicTypeDirective
documentation:
A directive which parses the type of a variadic typeable directive, with format:
  type-directive ::= type(typeable-directive)

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.VariableDirective

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.VariableDirective.name
documentation: The variable name. This is only used for error message reporting.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.VariableDirective.index
documentation: Index of the variable(operand or result) definition.

A variable directive, with the following format:
  variable-directive ::= dollar-ident
The directive will request a space to be printed after.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.AttrDictDirective

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.AttrDictDirective.with_keyword
documentation: If this is set, the format starts with the `attributes` keyword.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.AttrDictDirective.reserved_attr_names
documentation:
The set of attributes that should not be printed.
These attributes are printed in other places in the format, and thus would be
printed twice otherwise.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.AttrDictDirective.print_properties
documentation:
If this is set, also print properties as part of the attribute dictionary.
This is used to keep compatibility with MLIR which allows that.

An attribute dictionary directive, with the following format:
   attr-dict-directive ::= attr-dict
   attr-dict-with-format-directive ::= `attributes` attr-dict
The directive (with and without the keyword) will always print a space before, and
will not request a space to be printed after.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.OperandVariable
documentation:
An operand variable, with the following format:
  operand-directive ::= dollar-ident
The directive will request a space to be printed after.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.VariadicOperandDirective
documentation:
Base class for typechecking.
A variadic operand directive cannot follow another variadic operand directive.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.VariadicOperandVariable
documentation:
A variadic operand variable, with the following format:
  operand-directive ::= ( percent-ident ( `,` percent-id )* )?
The directive will request a space to be printed after.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.OptionalOperandVariable
documentation:
An optional operand variable, with the following format:
  operand-directive ::= ( percent-ident )?
The directive will request a space to be printed after.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.OperandsOrResultDirective

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.OperandsOrResultDirective.variadic_index
documentation:
Represents the position of a (single) variadic variable, with the boolean
representing whether it is optional
Base class for the 'operands' and 'results' directives.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.OperandsDirective
documentation:
An operands directive, with the following format:
  operands-directive ::= operands
Prints each operand of the operation, inserting a comma between each.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.ResultVariable
documentation:
An result variable, with the following format:
  result-directive ::= dollar-ident
This directive can not be used for parsing and printing directly, as result
parsing is not handled by the custom operation parser.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.VariadicResultVariable
documentation:
A variadic result variable, with the following format:
  result-directive ::= percent-ident (( `,` percent-id )* )?
This directive can not be used for parsing and printing directly, as result
parsing is not handled by the custom operation parser.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.OptionalResultVariable
documentation:
An optional result variable, with the following format:
  result-directive ::= ( percent-ident )?
This directive can not be used for parsing and printing directly, as result
parsing is not handled by the custom operation parser.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.ResultsDirective
documentation:
A results directive, with the following format:
  results-directive ::= results
A typeable directive which processes the result types of the operation.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.RegionDirective
documentation:
Baseclass to help keep typechecking simple.
RegionDirective is for any RegionVariable, which are all OptionallyParsable.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.VariadicRegionDirective
documentation:
Base class for typechecking.
A variadic region directive cannot follow another variadic region directive.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.RegionVariable
documentation:
A region variable, with the following format:
  region-directive ::= dollar-ident
The directive will request a space to be printed after.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.VariadicRegionVariable
documentation:
A variadic region variable, with the following format:
  region-directive ::= dollar-ident

The directive will request a space to be printed after.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.OptionalRegionVariable
documentation:
An optional region variable, with the following format:
  region-directive ::= dollar-ident
The directive will request a space to be printed after.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.VariadicSuccessorDirective
documentation:
Base class for type checking.
A variadic successor directive cannot follow another variadic successor directive.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.SuccessorVariable
documentation:
A successor variable, with the following format:
  successor-directive ::= dollar-ident
The directive will request a space to be printed after.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.VariadicSuccessorVariable
documentation:
A variadic successor variable, with the following format:
  successor-directive ::= dollar-ident
The directive will request a space to be printed after.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.OptionalSuccessorVariable
documentation:
An optional successor variable, with the following format:
  successor-directive ::= dollar-ident
The directive will request a space to be printed after.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.AttributeVariable

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.AttributeVariable.name
documentation: The attribute name as it should be in the attribute or property dictionary.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.AttributeVariable.is_property
documentation: Should this attribute be put in the attribute or property dictionary.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.AttributeVariable.unique_base
documentation: The known base class of the Attribute, if any.

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.AttributeVariable.unique_type
documentation: The known type of the Attribute, if any.

An attribute variable, with the following format:
  result-directive ::= dollar-ident
The directive will request a space to be printed right after.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.DefaultValuedAttributeVariable
documentation:
An attribute variable with default value, with the following format:
  result-directive ::= dollar-ident
The directive will request a space to be printed right after.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.OptionalAttributeVariable
documentation:
An optional attribute variable, with the following format:
  operand-directive ::= ( percent-ident )?
The directive will request a space to be printed after.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.OptionalUnitAttrVariable
documentation:
An optional UnitAttr variable that holds no value and derives its meaning from its existence. Holds a parse
and print method to reflect this.

  operand-directive ::= (`unit_attr` unit_attr^)?

Also see: https://mlir.llvm.org/docs/DefiningDialects/Operations/#unit-attributes

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.WhitespaceDirective

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.WhitespaceDirective.whitespace
documentation: The whitespace that should be printed.

A whitespace directive, with the following format:
      whitespace-directive ::= `
` | ` ` | ``
    This directive is only applied during printing, and has no effect during
    parsing.
    The directive will not request any space to be printed after.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.PunctuationDirective

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.PunctuationDirective.punctuation
documentation: The punctuation that should be printed/parsed.

A punctuation directive, with the following format:
  punctuation-directive ::= punctuation
The directive will request a space to be printed right after, unless the punctuation
is `<`, `(`, `{`, or `[`.
It will also print a space before if a space is requested, and that the punctuation
is neither `>`, `)`, `}`, `]`, or `,` if the last element was a punctuation, and
additionally neither `<`, `(`, `}`, `]`, if the last element was not a punctuation.

classes.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.KeywordDirective

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.declarative_assembly_format.KeywordDirective.keyword
documentation: The identifier that should be printed.

A keyword directive, with the following format:
  keyword-directive ::= bare-ident
The directive expects a specific identifier, and will request a space to be printed
after.

modules.name: xdsl.dialects.snitch
documentation:
This dialect provides operations to target features of the Snitch[1]
streaming architecture based on custom extensions to the RISC-V ISA.
This dialect works on 'riscv' types directly as all arguments are of
'riscv.reg<>' type and it is meant to be as close as possible to the asm
that aims at generating.

[1] https://pulp-platform.github.io/snitch/publications

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.snitch.SnitchResources
documentation: Bounds for resources provided by the Snitch architecture.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.snitch.SsrSetDimensionConfigOperation
documentation:
A base class for Snitch operations that set a
configuration value for a specific dimension handled by a streamer.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.snitch.SsrSetStreamConfigOperation
documentation:
A base class for Snitch operations that set a
configuration value for a streamer.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.snitch.SsrSetDimensionBoundOp
documentation:
Set the bound for one of the dimensions handled by a
specific streamer.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.snitch.SsrSetDimensionStrideOp
documentation:
Set the stride for one of the dimensions handled by a

documentation:
Marks the Python implementation of an xDSL `Operation` instance, to be used
by an `Interpreter`. The Interpreter will fetch the Python values

the `args` parameter. The returned values are assigned to the `results`

modules.name: xdsl.dialects.ptr
documentation: This is a port of the 'ptr' dialect proposed in this thread https://discourse.llvm.org/t/rfc-ptr-dialect-modularizing-ptr-ops-in-the-llvm-dialect/75142/21
Main parts of the dialect has already been agreed upon but only the basic datatype is implemented upstream.
When the upstream implementation is merged we should fix our implementation to be consistent with the mlir version.
Current diviations:
(1) Name of the dialect should be changed: ptr_xdsl -> ptr. We currently chose another name to avoid conflicts when loading our dialect to mlir-opt.
(2) There is no ptr.to_ptr operation currently proposed, but there is a memref.to_ptr. We do this so that we can feed the results of convert-memref-to-ptr pass without any conflict.

modules.name: xdsl.dialects.snitch_stream
documentation:
A dialect that represents at the highest level of abstraction the capabilities of the
[Snitch](https://github.com/pulp-platform/snitch_cluster) accelerator core, as used in
[Occamy](https://github.com/pulp-platform/occamy) and others.

The core aims to optimise for performance per watt, by replacing caches and branch
prediction logic with streaming registers and fixed-repetition loops. This dialect models
the streaming functionality of the Snitch core.

`snitch_stream.stride_pattern_type` represents a specification of the order in which
elements of a streamed region of memory will be read from or written to.

`snitch_stream.stride_pattern` creates a value storing the above specification.

`snitch_stream.streaming_region` encapsulates a region of code where the streams are
valid. According to the Snitch ABI, within this region, the registers `ft0` to `ftn`,
where `n` is the number of streaming registers, have a restricted functionality. If the
register is configured as a readable stream register, then it cannot be written to, and
if the register is configured as a writable stream register, then it cannot be read from.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.snitch_stream.StridePattern

methods.name: xdsl.dialects.snitch_stream.StridePattern.simplified
documentation:
Return a stride pattern that specifies the same iteration space, but with folded
perfectly nested outermost loops.

e.g.

```
stride_pattern<ub = [2, 3, 4], strides = [12, 4, 1]>
->
stride_pattern<ub = [24], strides = [1]
```

class_variables.name: xdsl.dialects.snitch_stream.StridePattern.repeat

Pointers to memory buffers that will be streamed. The corresponding stride pattern

class_variables.name: xdsl.dialects.snitch_stream.StreamingRegionOp.outputs
documentation:
Pointers to memory buffers that will be streamed. The corresponding stride pattern
defines the order in which the elements of the input buffers will be written to.

class_variables.name: xdsl.dialects.snitch_stream.StreamingRegionOp.stride_patterns
documentation:
Stride patterns that define the order of the input and output streams. If there is
one stride pattern, and more inputs and outputs, the stride pattern is applied to all
the streams.

An operation that creates streams from access patterns, which are only available to
read from and write to within the body of the operation.

According to the Snitch ABI, within this region, the registers `ft0` to `ftn`,
where `n` is the number of streaming registers, have a restricted functionality. If the
register is configured as a readable stream register, then it cannot be written to, and
if the register is configured as a writable stream register, then it cannot be read from.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.bufferization.TensorFromMemrefConstraint
documentation:
Converts an input memref constraint to the corresponding tensor constraint, i.e. the constraints
on element type and shape are the same as the input constraint, but the attribute is verified to be

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.bufferization.AllocTensorOp
documentation:
`bufferization.alloc_tensor` materializes an uninitialized tensor with a
given shape (dynamic or static). It always bufferizes to a new buffer
allocation of the given shape. The optional `copy` operand specifies the
contents of the tensors. If no `copy` operand is specified, reading from the
result of an `alloc_tensor` op yields an undefined value.

If `copy` is specified, no dynamic sizes should be passed, since they are
the same as the dynamic sizes of the `copy` operand.

`alloc_tensor` is a helper op for bufferization. The operation is provided
as an anchor that marks the beginning of a new tensor SSA use-def chain. It
can be used to control in-place bufferization decisions during One-Shot
Bufferize: The bufferized result of a `bufferization.alloc_tensor` does not
alias with any other buffer, so it can be used to resolve read-after-write
conflicts that would have been introduced by the in-place bufferization of
another op.

The optional `memory_space` attribute specifies the memory space when
bufferizing this op. The memory space is inferred from `copy` if specified.
If neither `copy` nor `memory_space` is specified, the default memory space
is used during bufferization.

The optional `size_hint` operand specifies the number of non-zero elements
for sparse tensors. The value of `size_hint` should be not less than 1 and
not larger than the linear size of the corresponding dense tensor type. If
this requirement is not met, the behavior of the operator is undefined.

Note: An `alloc_tensor` with a `copy` should also be expressed as an
`alloc_tensor` without `copy`, followed by a `copy_tensor`.

https://mlir.llvm.org/docs/Dialects/BufferizationOps/#bufferizationalloc_tensor-bufferizationalloctensorop

class_variables.name: xdsl.irdl.operations.AttrSizedSegments.as_property
documentation: Name of the attribute containing the segment sizes.

Expect an attribute on the operation that contains the segment sizes of the
operand, result, region, or successor lists.
For instance, the list `[a, b, c, d]` with segment sizes `[1, 3]` will result
in the `[a], [b, c, d]` lists.
The attribute must be a dense array of `i32`, its lenght must be equal to the
number of segments (e.g. the number of operand definitions), and its sum must
be equal to the number of elements in the list (e.g. the number of operands).

modules.name: xdsl.dialects.riscv_scf
documentation: RISC-V SCF dialect

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.riscv_scf.ForOp
documentation: A for loop, counting up from lb to ub by step each iteration.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.riscv_scf.RofOp
documentation:
Reverse Order For loop.

MLIR's for loops have the constraint of always executing from lb to ub,
so in order to express loops that count down from ub to lb, the rof op
is needed.

Rof has the semantics of going from ub to lb, decrementing by step each time.
The implicit constraints are that lb < ub, and step > 0.

In order to convert a for to a rof, one needs to switch lb and ub.
(for the normalized case that (ub - lb) % step == 0)

methods.name: xdsl.dialects.riscv_scf.WhileOp.verify_

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.test.TestOp
documentation:
This operation can produce an arbitrary number of SSAValues with arbitrary
types. It is used in filecheck testing to reduce to artificial dependencies
on other dialects (i.e. dependencies that only come from the structure of
the test rather than the actual dialect).

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.test.TestTermOp
documentation:
This operation can produce an arbitrary number of SSAValues with arbitrary
types. It is used in filecheck testing to reduce to artificial dependencies
on other dialects (i.e. dependencies that only come from the structure of
the test rather than the actual dialect).
Its main difference from TestOp is that it satisfies the IsTerminator trait
and can be used as a block terminator operation.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.test.TestPureOp
documentation:
This operation can produce an arbitrary number of SSAValues with arbitrary
types. It is used in filecheck testing to reduce to artificial dependencies
on other dialects (i.e. dependencies that only come from the structure of
the test rather than the actual dialect).
Its main difference from TestOp is that it satisfies the Pure trait, so we can
test region CSE - this op assumes no side effects nowhere in its regions.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.test.TestReadOp
documentation:
This operation can produce an arbitrary number of SSAValues with arbitrary
types. It is used in filecheck testing to reduce to artificial dependencies
on other dialects (i.e. dependencies that only come from the structure of
the test rather than the actual dialect).
Its main difference from TestOp is that it satisfies the MemoryReadEffect trait,
so we can test CSE - this op assumes it always and only reads.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.test.TestWriteOp
documentation:
This operation can produce an arbitrary number of SSAValues with arbitrary
types. It is used in filecheck testing to reduce to artificial dependencies
on other dialects (i.e. dependencies that only come from the structure of
the test rather than the actual dialect).
Its main difference from TestOp is that it satisfies the MemoryReadEffect trait,
so we can test CSE - this op assumes it always and only writes.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.test.TestType
documentation:
This attribute is used for testing in places where any attribute can be
used. This allows reducing the artificial dependencies on attributes from
other dialects.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.qssa.QubitAttr
documentation: Type for a single qubit.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.qssa.QssaBase

methods.name: xdsl.dialects.qssa.QssaBase.ref_op
documentation: Build corresponding qref operation.

methods.name: xdsl.dialects.qssa.QssaBase.is_gate
documentation:
Is this operation a gate?
Qssa gates represent standard quantum logic gates.
They should have an equal number of qubit-typed operands and results.
Their results must be rewired when converting to the qref dialect.

Base class for qssa operations, with methods to help convert to the qref dialect.

Invariant:
self.is_gate == self.ref_op().is_gate

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.gpu.HostRegisterOp
documentation:
This op maps the provided host buffer into the device address space.

This operation may not be supported in every environment, there is not yet a way to
check at runtime whether this feature is supported.
Writes from the host are guaranteed to be visible to device kernels that are launched
afterwards. Writes from the device are guaranteed to be visible on the host after
synchronizing with the device kernel completion.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.gpu.HostUnregisterOp
documentation: Unregisters a memref for access from device.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.gpu.LaunchFuncOp
documentation:
Launch a kernel function on the specified grid of thread blocks. gpu.launch
operations are lowered to gpu.launch_func operations by outlining the kernel body
into a function in a dedicated module, which reflects the separate compilation
process. The kernel function is required to have the gpu.kernel attribute. The
module containing the kernel function is required to be a gpu.module. And finally,
the module containing the kernel module (which thus cannot be the top-level module)
is required to have the gpu.container_module attribute. The gpu.launch_func operation
has a symbol attribute named kernel to identify the fully specified kernel function
to launch (both the gpu.module and func).

The gpu.launch_func supports async dependencies: the kernel does not start executing
until the ops producing those async dependencies have completed.

By default, the host implicitly blocks until kernel execution has completed. If
the async keyword is present, the host does not block but instead a !gpu.async.token
is returned. Other async GPU ops can take this token as dependency.

The operation requires at least the grid and block sizes along the x,y,z dimensions
as arguments. When a lower-dimensional kernel is required, unused sizes must be
explicitly set to 1.

The remaining operands are optional. The first optional operand corresponds to the
amount of dynamic shared memory a kernel's workgroup should be allocated; when this
operand is not present, a zero size is assumed.

The remaining operands if present are passed as arguments to the kernel function.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.FastMathFlagsAttr
documentation: arith.fastmath is a mirror of LLVMs fastmath flags.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.SignlessIntegerBinaryOperation
documentation: A generic base class for arith's binary operations on signless integers.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.SignlessIntegerBinaryOperationWithOverflow
documentation:
A generic base class for arith's binary operations on signless integers which
can overflow.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.FloatingPointLikeBinaryOperation
documentation: A generic base class for arith's binary operations on floats.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.AddUIExtendedOp
documentation:
An add operation on an unsigned representation of integers that returns a flag
indicating if the result overflowed.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.MulExtendedBase
documentation: Base class for extended multiplication operations.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.MulUIExtendedOp
documentation: Extended unsigned integer multiplication operation.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.MulSIExtendedOp
documentation: Extended unsigned integer multiplication operation.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.DivUIOp
documentation:
Unsigned integer division. Rounds towards zero. Treats the leading bit as
the most significant, i.e. for `i16` given two's complement representation,
`6 / -2 = 6 / (2^16 - 2) = 0`.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.DivSIOp
documentation:
Signed integer division. Rounds towards zero. Treats the leading bit as
sign, i.e. `6 / -2 = -3`.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.FloorDivSIOp
documentation: Signed floor integer division. Rounds towards negative infinity i.e. `5 / -2 = -3`.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.ShLIOp
documentation:
The `shli` operation shifts an integer value to the left by a variable
amount. The low order bits are filled with zeros.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.ShRUIOp
documentation:
The `shrui` operation shifts an integer value to the right by a variable
amount. The integer is interpreted as unsigned. The high order bits are
always filled with zeros.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.ShRSIOp
documentation:
The `shrsi` operation shifts an integer value to the right by a variable
amount. The integer is interpreted as signed. The high order bits in the
output are filled with copies of the most-significant bit of the shifted
value (which means that the sign of the value is preserved).

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.ComparisonOperation
documentation:
A generic comparison operation, operation definitions inherit this class.

The first argument to these comparison operations is the type of comparison
being performed, the following comparisons are supported:

-   equal (mnemonic: `"eq"`; integer value: `0`)
-   not equal (mnemonic: `"ne"`; integer value: `1`)
-   signed less than (mnemonic: `"slt"`; integer value: `2`)
-   signed less than or equal (mnemonic: `"sle"`; integer value: `3`)
-   signed greater than (mnemonic: `"sgt"`; integer value: `4`)
-   signed greater than or equal (mnemonic: `"sge"`; integer value: `5`)
-   unsigned less than (mnemonic: `"ult"`; integer value: `6`)
-   unsigned less than or equal (mnemonic: `"ule"`; integer value: `7`)
-   unsigned greater than (mnemonic: `"ugt"`; integer value: `8`)
-   unsigned greater than or equal (mnemonic: `"uge"`; integer value: `9`)

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.CmpiOp
documentation:
The cmpi operation is a generic comparison for integer-like types. Its two
arguments can be integers, vectors or tensors thereof as long as their types
match. The operation produces an i1 for the former case, a vector or a
tensor of i1 with the same shape as inputs in the other cases.

The result is `1` if the comparison is true and `0` otherwise. For vector or
tensor operands, the comparison is performed elementwise and the element of
the result indicates whether the comparison is true for the operand elements
with the same indices as those of the result.

Example:

// Custom form of scalar "signed less than" comparison.
%x = arith.cmpi slt, %lhs, %rhs : i32

// Generic form of the same operation.
%x = "arith.cmpi"(%lhs, %rhs) {predicate = 2 : i64} : (i32, i32) -> i1

// Custom form of vector equality comparison.
%x = arith.cmpi eq, %lhs, %rhs : vector<4xi64>

// Generic form of the same operation.
%x = "arith.cmpi"(%lhs, %rhs) {predicate = 0 : i64}
    : (vector<4xi64>, vector<4xi64>) -> vector<4xi1>

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.CmpfOp
documentation:
The cmpf operation compares its two operands according to the float
comparison rules and the predicate specified by the respective attribute.
The predicate defines the type of comparison: (un)orderedness, (in)equality
and signed less/greater than (or equal to) as well as predicates that are
always true or false.  The operands must have the same type, and this type
must be a float type, or a vector or tensor thereof.  The result is an i1,
or a vector/tensor thereof having the same shape as the inputs. Unlike cmpi,
the operands are always treated as signed. The u prefix indicates
*unordered* comparison, not unsigned comparison, so "une" means unordered or
not equal. For the sake of readability by humans, custom assembly form for
the operation uses a string-typed attribute for the predicate.  The value of
this attribute corresponds to lower-cased name of the predicate constant,
e.g., "one" means "ordered not equal".  The string representation of the
attribute is merely a syntactic sugar and is converted to an integer
attribute by the parser.

Example:

%r1 = arith.cmpf oeq, %0, %1 : f32
%r2 = arith.cmpf ult, %0, %1 : tensor<42x42xf64>
%r3 = "arith.cmpf"(%0, %1) {predicate: 0} : (f8, f8) -> i1

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.SelectOp

methods.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.SelectOp.verify_
documentation: TODO replace with trait

class_variables.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.SelectOp.cond
documentation: should be unsigned

The `arith.select` operation chooses one value based on a binary condition
supplied as its first operand. If the value of the first operand is `1`,
the second operand is chosen, otherwise the third operand is chosen.
The second and the third operand must have the same type.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.MaximumfOp
documentation:
Returns the maximum of the two arguments, treating -0.0 as less than +0.0.
If one of the arguments is NaN, then the result is also NaN.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.MaxnumfOp
documentation:
Returns the maximum of the two arguments.
If the arguments are -0.0 and +0.0, then the result is either of them.
If one of the arguments is NaN, then the result is the other argument.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.MinimumfOp
documentation:
Returns the minimum of the two arguments, treating -0.0 as less than +0.0.
If one of the arguments is NaN, then the result is also NaN.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.arith.MinnumfOp
documentation:
Returns the minimum of the two arguments. If the arguments are -0.0 and +0.0, then the result is either of them.
If one of the arguments is NaN, then the result is the other argument.

functions.name: xdsl.dialects.onnx.verify_unidirectional_broadcast_shape
documentation: Returns a unidirectional broadcastable shape

functions.name: xdsl.dialects.onnx.verify_multidirectional_broadcast_shape
documentation: Returns a multidirectional broadcastable shape

functions.name: xdsl.dialects.onnx.unidirectional_broadcast_shape
documentation:
In ONNX, tensor B is unidirectional broadcastable to tensor A if one of the following is true:

1. Tensor A and B both have exactly the same shape.
2. Tensor A and B all have the same number of dimensions and
the length of each dimensions is either a common length or B's length is 1.

3.Tensor B has too few dimensions, and B can have its shapes prepended with a dimension of length 1 to satisfy
property 2.

When unidirectional broadcasting happens, the output's shape is the same as the shape of A (i.e.,
the larger shape of two input tensors)

https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/main/docs/Broadcasting.md

functions.name: xdsl.dialects.onnx.multidirectional_broadcast_shape
documentation:
In ONNX, a set of tensors are multidirectional broadcastable to the same shape if one of the following is true:

1.The tensors all have exactly the same shape.
2.The tensors all have the same number of dimensions and the length of each dimensions is either a common length or 1.
3.The tensors that have too few dimensions can have their shapes prepended with a dimension of length 1 to satisfy property 2.

https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/main/docs/Broadcasting.md

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.onnx.ElementwiseBinOpBase
documentation: Base class for element-wise binary operations on tensors with Numpy-style broadcasting.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.onnx.ReluOp
documentation:
Relu takes one input data (Tensor) and produces one output data (Tensor) where the rectified linear function,
 y = max(0, x), is applied to the tensor elementwise.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.onnx.GemmOp
documentation:
General Matrix multiplication: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Basic_Linear_Algebra_Subprograms#Level_3
A' = transpose(A) if transA else A
B' = transpose(B) if transB else B
Compute Y = alpha * A' * B' + beta * C,
where input tensor A has shape (M, K) or (K, M), input tensor B has shape (K, N) or (N, K),
input tensor C is broadcastable to shape (M, N), and output tensor Y has shape (M, N).

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.onnx.ReshapeOp
documentation:
Reshape the input tensor similar to numpy.reshape.
First input is the data tensor, second input is a shape tensor which specifies the output shape. It outputs the reshaped tensor.
At most one dimension of the new shape can be -1. In this case, the value is
inferred from the size of the tensor and the remaining dimensions. A dimension
could also be 0, in which case the actual dimension value is unchanged (i.e. taken
from the input tensor). Shape (second input) could be an empty shape, which means converting to a scalar.
The input tensor's shape and the output tensor's shape are required to have the same number of elements.

 Attributes:
    - allowzero  int (default is 0):  By default, when any value in the 'shape' input is equal to zero
 the corresponding dimension value is copied from the input tensor dynamically. allowzero=1 indicates that if any
 value in the 'shape' input is set to zero, the zero value is honoured, similar to NumPy.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.onnx.AbsOp
documentation:
Absolute takes one input data (Tensor) and produces one output data (Tensor) where absolute value,
y = abs(x), is applied to the tensor elementwise.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.onnx.ConvOp
documentation:
The convolution operator consumes an input tensor and a filter, and computes the output.

Attributes:

- auto_pad  string (default is NOTSET): auto_pad must be either NOTSET, SAME_UPPER, SAME_LOWER or
VALID. Where default value is NOTSET, which means explicit padding is used. SAME_UPPER or SAME_LOWER mean pad the
input so that output_shape[i] = ceil(input_shape[i] / strides[i]) for each axis i. The padding is split between
the two sides equally or almost equally (depending on whether it is even or odd). In case the padding is an odd
number, the extra padding is added at the end for SAME_UPPER and at the beginning for SAME_LOWER.

-dilations list of ints: dilation value along each spatial axis of the filter. If not present, the dilation
defaults is 1 along each spatial axis.

-group int (default is '1'): number of groups input channels and output channels are divided into.

-kernel_shape list of ints: The shape of the convolution kernel. If not present, should be inferred from input W.

-pads list of ints: Padding for the beginning and ending along each spatial axis, it can take any value greater
than or equal to 0. The value represent the number of pixels added to the beginning and end part of the
corresponding axis. `pads` format should be as follow [x1_begin, x2_begin...x1_end, x2_end,...], where xi_begin
the number of pixels added at the beginning of axis `i` and xi_end, the number of pixels added at the end of axis
`i`. This attribute cannot be used simultaneously with auto_pad attribute. If not present, the padding defaults
to 0 along start and end of each spatial axis.

-strides list of ints: Stride along each spatial axis. If not present, the stride defaults is 1 along each spatial axis.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.onnx.ConstantOp
documentation:
Produce a constant tensor.

Exactly one of the provided attributes, either value, sparse_value, or value_* must be specified.

Attributes:
- sparse_value: sparse_tensor
The value for the elements of the output tensor in sparse format. (currently unsupported)
- value : tensor
 The value for the elements of the output tensor.
- value_float: float
 The value for the sole element for the scalar, float32, output tensor.
- value_floats: list of floats
 The values for the elements for the 1D, float32, output tensor.
- value_int : int
 The value for the sole element for the scalar, int64, output tensor.
- value_ints : list of ints
 The values for the elements for the 1D, int64, output tensor.
- value_string : string
 The value for the sole element for the scalar, UTF-8 string, output tensor.
- value_strings: list of strings
 The values for the elements for the 1D, UTF-8 string, output tensor.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.onnx.MaxPoolSingleOutOp
documentation:
ONNX MaxPool operation with a single output.

 Attributes:

- auto_pad string (default is 'NOTSET'):  auto_pad must be either NOTSET, SAME_UPPER, SAME_LOWER or
VALID. Where default value is NOTSET, which means explicit padding is used. SAME_UPPER or SAME_LOWER mean pad the
input so that output_shape[i] = ceil(input_shape[i] / strides[i]) for each axis i. The padding is split between
the two sides equally or almost equally (depending on whether it is even or odd). In case the padding is an odd
number, the extra padding is added at the end for SAME_UPPER and at the beginning for SAME_LOWER.

- ceil_mode  int (default is '1'): Whether to use ceil or floor (default) to compute the output shape.

- dilations list of ints: Dilation value along each spatial axis of filter.

- kernel_shape list of ints: The size of the kernel along each axis.

- pads list of ints: Padding for the beginning and ending along each spatial axis, it can take any value greater
than or equal to 0. The value represent the number of pixels added to the beginning and end part of the
corresponding axis. `pads` format should be as follow [x1_begin, x2_begin...x1_end, x2_end,...], where xi_begin
the number of pixels added at the beginning of axis `i` and xi_end, the number of pixels added at the end of axis
`i`. This attribute cannot be used simultaneously with auto_pad attribute. If not present, the padding defaults
to 0 along start and end of each spatial axis.

- storage_order int (default is '0') : The storage order of the tensor. 0 is row major, and 1 is column major.
This attribute is used only to convert an n-tuple index value into a single integer value for producing the
second output.

- strides list of ints: Stride along each spatial axis. If not present, the stride defaults to 1 along each spatial axis

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.onnx.EntryPointOp
documentation:
Indicate ONNX entry point
The "onnx.EntryPoint" function indicates the main entry point of ONNX model.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.onnx.MatMulOp
documentation:
The operation MatMul performs matrix multiplication between two input matrices, A and B, and returns the result as matrix Y.
Matrix multiplication is a fundamental operation in linear algebra, where each element of the resulting matrix Y is computed by taking the
dot product of the corresponding row of matrix A and column of matrix B.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.onnx.TransposeOp
documentation:
The transpose_tensor function takes a tensor as input and returns its transpose.
Transposing a tensor means flipping its dimensions, so that rows become columns and vice versa.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.onnx.SqueezeOp
documentation:
Squeeze the input tensor along the specified axes.

Squeezing a tensor removes dimensions of size 1, effectively reducing the rank of the tensor and collapsing those dimensions.
This operation is particularly useful for removing unnecessary singleton dimensions, which may arise from broadcasting or previous operations.

Args:
    input_tensor: The input tensor to be squeezed. This tensor should be a multi-dimensional array-like object.
    axes: A list of axes along which to squeeze the tensor. If provided, only the specified axes will be squeezed. If not provided, all dimensions of size 1 will be squeezed.

Returns:
    output_tensor: The squeezed tensor.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.onnx.SigmoidOp
documentation:
Applies the sigmoid function element-wise to all elements of the input tensor.
The sigmoid function, denoted by sigma(x), is a common mathematical function used in machine learning and neural networks. It is defined as:
sigma(x) = 1 / (1 + e^-x)
where e is the base of the natural logarithm. The sigmoid function maps any real-valued number to the range of [0, 1].
The sigmoid function is used as an activation function.

Args:
- input_tensor (TensorType): The input tensor to which the sigmoid function will be applied.

Returns:
- output_tensor (TensorType): The output tensor after applying the sigmoid function element-wise to the input tensor.

methods.name: xdsl.dialects.linalg.GenericOp.get_loops_to_shapes_map
documentation:
Returns a map to answer the question: "given an iteration space over
the codomain, what are the subshapes of the operands involved in the
computation".
The default behavior is to just concatenate all the indexing maps.

methods.name: xdsl.dialects.linalg.GenericOp.get_shapes_to_loops_map
documentation:
Returns a map to answer the question: "Given a list of operand ranges,
what is the subportion of the iteration space involved in the
computation". This is the inverse problem of `get_loops_to_shapes_map`.
Return the empty AffineMap when such an AffineMap cannot be
constructed. The default behavior is based on a very simple inference
procedure that only works with permutation affine maps. A more advanced
Tensor-Comprehension like inference is possible but has proven to be
ambiguous in unfavorable case. A safer and more robust alternative is
to allow each op to define its own AffineMap.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.linalg.NamedOpBase

methods.name: xdsl.dialects.linalg.NamedOpBase.body_arg_types
documentation: Return the element types of the arguments of the body of this operation
Abstract base class for named ops with hidden region.

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.linalg.AddOp
documentation:
Adds two tensors elementwise.

See https://mlir.llvm.org/docs/Dialects/Linalg/#linalgadd-linalgaddop

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.linalg.SubOp
documentation:
Subtracts two tensors elementwise.

See https://mlir.llvm.org/docs/Dialects/Linalg/#linalgsub-linalgsubop

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.linalg.FillOp
documentation:
Fills the output tensor with the given value.

Works for arbitrary ranked output tensors since the operation performs scalar accesses
only and is thus rank polymorphic. Numeric casting is performed on the value operand,
promoting it to the same data type as the output.

See https://mlir.llvm.org/docs/Dialects/Linalg/#linalgfill-linalgfillop

`pdl.replace` operations are used within `pdl.rewrite` regions to specify
that an input operation should be marked as replaced. The semantics of this
operation correspond with the `replaceOp` method on a `PatternRewriter`. The
set of replacement values can be either:
* a single `Operation` (`replOperation` should be populated)
  - The operation will be replaced with the results of this operation.
* a set of `Value`s (`replValues` should be populated)
  - The operation will be replaced with these values.

methods.name: xdsl.dialects.llvm.GEPOp.__init__
documentation:
A basic constructor for the GEPOp.

Pass the GEP_USE_SSA_VAL magic value in place of each constant
index that you want to be read from an SSA value.

Take a look at `from_mixed_indices` for something without
magic values.

methods.name: xdsl.dialects.llvm.GEPOp.from_mixed_indices
documentation:
This is a helper function that accepts a mixed list of SSA values and const
indices. It will automatically construct the correct indices and ssa_indices
lists from that.

You can call this using [1, 2, some_ssa_val, 3] as the indices array.

Other than that, this behaves exactly the same as `.get`

llvm.getelementptr is an instruction to do pointer arithmetic by
adding/subtracting offsets from a pointer.

The llvm.getelementptr is documented in various places online:

LLVM documentation: https://www.llvm.org/docs/GetElementPtr.html
A good blogpost: https://blog.yossarian.net/2020/09/19/LLVMs-getelementptr-by-example
MLIR documentation: https://mlir.llvm.org/docs/Dialects/LLVM/#llvmgetelementptr-mlirllvmgepop

Note that the first two discuss *LLVM IRs* GEP operation, not the MLIR one.
The semantics are the same, but the structure used by MLIR is not well
documented (yet) and the syntax is a bit different.

Here we focus on MLIRs GEP operation:

%res = llvm.getelementptr %ptr  [1, 2, %val]
                          ^^^^   ^^^^^^^^^^
                          input   indices

The central point to understanding GEP is that:
> GEP never dereferences, it only does math on the given pointer

It *always* returns a pointer to the element "selected" that is some
number of bytes offset from the input pointer:

`result = ptr + x` for some x parametrized by the arguments

## Examples:

Given the following pointer:

%ptr : llvm.ptr<llvm.struct<(i32, i32, llvm.array<2xi32>)>>

The following indices point to the following things:

[0]      -> The first element of the pointer, so a pointer to the struct:
            llvm.ptr<llvm.struct<(i32, i32, llvm.array<2xi32>)>>

[1]      -> The *next* element of the pointer, useful if the
            pointer points to a list of structs.
            Equivalent to (ptr + 1), so points to
            llvm.ptr<llvm.struct<(i32, i32, llvm.array<2xi32>)>>

[0,0]    -> The first member of the first struct:
            llvm.ptr<i32>

[1,0]    -> The first member of the *second* struct pointed to by ptr
            (can result in out-of-bounds access if the ptr only points to a single struct)
            llvm.ptr<i32>

[0,2]    -> The third member of the first struct.
            llvm.ptr<llvm.array<2,i32>>

[0,2,0]  -> The first entry of the array that is the third member of
            the first struct pointed to by our ptr.
            llvm.ptr<i32>

[0,0,1]  -> Invalid! The first element of the first struct has no "sub-elements"!


Here is an example of invalid GEP operation parameters:

Given a different pointer to the example above:

%ptr : llvm.ptr<llvm.struct<(llvm.ptr<i32>, i32)>>

Note the two pointers, one to the struct, one in the struct.

We can do math on the first pointer:

[0]      -> First struct
            llvm.ptr<llvm.struct<(llvm.ptr<i32>, i32)>>

[0,1]    -> Second member of first struct
            llvm.ptr<i32>

[0,0]    -> First member of the first struct
            llvm.ptr<llvm.ptr<i32>>

[0,0,3]  -> Invalid! In order to find the fourth element in the pointer
            it would need to be dereferenced! GEP can't do that!

Expressed in "C", this would equate to:

# address of first struct
(ptr + 0)

# address of first field of first struct
&((ptr + 0)->elm0)
           ^^^^^^
           Even though it looks like it, we are not actually
           dereferencing ptr here.

# address of fourth element:
&(((ptr + 0)->elm0 + 3))
            ^^^^^^^^^^
            This actually dereferences (ptr + 0) to access elm0!

Which translates to roughly this MLIR code:

%elm0_addr   = llvm.gep %ptr[0,0]   : (!llvm.ptr<...>) -> !llvm.ptr<!llvm.ptr<i32>>
%elm0        = llvm.load %elm0_addr : (!llvm.ptr<llvm.ptr<i32>>) -> !llvm.ptr<i32>
%elm0_3_addr = llvm.gep %elm0[3]    : !llvm.ptr<i32> -> !llvm.ptr<i32>

Here the necessary dereferencing is very visible, as %elm0_3_addr is only
accessible through an `llvm.load` on %elm0_addr.

modules.name: xdsl.dialects.experimental.dmp
documentation:
A dialect for handling distributed memory parallelism (DMP).

This is xDSL only for now.

This dialect aims to provide the tools necessary to facilitate the creation
and lowering of stencil (and other) computations in a manner that
makes them run on node clusters.

functions.name: xdsl.dialects.experimental.dmp._flat_face_exchanges_for_dim
documentation:
Generate the two exchange delcarations to exchange the faces on the
axis "axis".

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.experimental.dmp.ExchangeDeclarationAttr

methods.name: xdsl.dialects.experimental.dmp.ExchangeDeclarationAttr.dims
documentation: number of dimensions of the grid

methods.name: xdsl.dialects.experimental.dmp.ExchangeDeclarationAttr.source_area
documentation:
Since a HaloExchangeDef by default specifies the area to receive into,
this method returns the area that should be read from.

This declares a region to be "halo-exchanged".
The semantics define that the region specified by offset and size
is the *received part*. To get the section that should be sent,
use the source_area() method to get the source area.

 - offset gives the coordinates from the origin of the stencil field.
 - size gives the size of the buffer to be exchanged.
 - source_offset gives a translation (n-d offset) where the data should be
   read from that is exchanged with the other node.
 - neighbor gives the offset in rank to the node this data is to be
   exchanged with

Example:

    offset = [4, 0]
    size   = [10, 1]
    source_offset = [0, 1]
    neighbor = -1

To visualize:
0   4         14
    xxxxxxxxxx    0
    oooooooooo    1

Where `x` signifies the area that should be received,
and `o` the area that should be read from.

This data will be exchanged with the node of rank (my_rank -1)

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.experimental.dmp.ShapeAttr

methods.name: xdsl.dialects.experimental.dmp.ShapeAttr.dims
documentation: Number of axis of the data (len(shape))

methods.name: xdsl.dialects.experimental.dmp.ShapeAttr.buff_size
documentation: Helpers for specific sizes:

methods.name: xdsl.dialects.experimental.dmp.ShapeAttr.print_parameters
documentation: parsing / printing

methods.name: xdsl.dialects.experimental.dmp.ShapeAttr.parse_parameters
documentation:
Parses the attribute, the format of it is:

#dmp.shape_with_halo<[a0,b0,c0,d0]x[a1,b1,c1,d1]x...>

so different from the way it's stored internally.

This decision was made to improve readability.

This represents shape information that is attached to halo operations.

On the terminology used:

In each dimension, we are given four points. We abbreviate them in
annotations to an, bn, cn, dn, with n being the dimension. In 2d, these
create the following pattern, higher dimensional examples can
be derived from this:

a1 b1          c1 d1
+--+-----------+--+ a0
|  |           |  |
+--+-----------+--+ b0
|  |           |  |
|  |           |  |
|  |           |  |
|  |           |  |
+--+-----------+--+ c0
|  |           |  |
+--+-----------+--+ d0

We can now name these points:

     - a: buffer_start
     - b: core_start
     - c: core_end
     - d: buffer_end

This class provides easy getters for these four.

We can also define some common sizes on this object:

    - buff_size(n) = dn - an
    - core_size(n) = cn - bn
    - halo_size(n, start) = bn - an
    - halo_size(n, end  ) = dn - cn

classes.name: xdsl.dialects.experimental.dmp.RankTopoAttr
documentation:
This attribute specifies the node layout used to distribute the computation.

dmp.grid<3x3> means nine ranks organized in a 3x3 grid.

This allows for higher-dimensional grids as well, e.g. dmp.grid<3x3x3> for
3-dimensional data.
